narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46.2% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your Year 1 school decile of 3—below the typical student's 5.71—and your Year 3 decile of 2, even lower than the average 5.53. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 and LSAT score of 35.5 also sit a bit under the dataset averages of 3.21 and 36.7, respectively, while your middle family income aligns closely with the norm around 3.47. As a full-time female student of white background, you match patterns seen in most applicants—43.6% female, 83.8% white, and 92.7% full-time—but these didn't offset the academic signals. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 1 decile, which nudged the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. Right behind it was your LSAT score, followed closely by your Year 3 decile, each contributing to that higher risk assessment in descending order of influence. Your family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status rounded out the factors, all leaning slightly toward the failure side but with less pull. Compared to typical students who pass, your early and later school deciles stand out as notably lower, putting you in a tougher spot academically than most. While no single feature dooms you, this combination painted a picture of elevated bar exam risk. To boost your future odds, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to push it above average, as it was the second-biggest factor. Strengthening any weak study habits reflected in your deciles could help through targeted prep courses. Ultimately, dedicated bar exam preparation tailored to your profile will be key to defying this prediction and succeeding. Keep pushing—you have the drive to change this story.
